text: The surgeon begins by taking down the end ileostomy, detaching the ileostomy from the abdominal wall.
The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel, carefully manipulating the intestinal loops to achieve adequate exposure and positioning for the next steps of the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the small bowel mesentery from the retroperitoneum, beginning in the right lower quadrant and advancing towards the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon displays the free edge of the small bowel mesentery alongside the terminal ileum, highlighting these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon shifts focus to the pelvic region, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon identifies the rectal stump, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues in the surgical field.
The surgeon enters the mesorectal plane, revealing the distinct tissue layers within the pelvic region.
The laparoscopic and transanal teams work simultaneously, coordinating their efforts in the surgical field.
The surgeon initiates the transanal approach by placing a purse string suture to close the rectum, preparing the area for further surgical steps.
The surgeon performs a full thickness proctotomy distal to the previously placed purse string suture, cutting through the rectal wall.
The surgeon makes the first incision on the anterior aspect of the rectum and proceeds with circumferential dissection around the rectal stump.
The surgeon performs a meticulous dissection to enter the mesorectal plane from below, using precise techniques to separate the tissue layers within the rectal area.
The laparoscopic team advances the circumferential dissection within the mesorectal plane, complementing the transanal approach.
The surgeon continues the dissection evenly across all quadrants, ensuring uniform progress without reaching the peritoneal reflection in any single quadrant before moving to the next.
The surgeon identifies the posterior vaginal wall during the dissection to avoid potential injuries, ensuring careful navigation around this anatomical structure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the neurovascular bundle, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The two dissection planes converge, allowing one team to visually or tactilely perceive the other team's approach during the procedure.
The light source from the other team is visible, aiding in the identification of the correct dissection plane and facilitating the surgical process.
Following the connection between the two dissection teams, the surgeon proceeds with circumferential dissection around the rectum, facilitating smoother tissue separation.
The specimen is completely freed following the final division, with no remaining attachments visible.
The surgeon extracts the specimen transanally, completing the removal process.
The surgeon places a purse string suture on the anal cuff through the rectal port, preparing the site for the positioning of the circular stapler.
The surgeon constructs an ileal J pouch through the old ostomy site, shaping the ileum into a reservoir for future intestinal continuity.
The surgeon places the anvil at the bottom of the ileal J pouch and returns the pouch to the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the ileal mesentery to reduce tension on the anastomosis. The circular stapler is advanced through the port and mated with the anvil, constructing the anastomosis.
The transanal team visually inspects the anastomosis for integrity, followed by the maturation of a diverting loop ileostomy at the old ostomy site.
